“We wanted to give something back. Our purpose is not only to raise funds, but to raise awareness, so no family ever has to experience what we did.”

News.title: Home Bargains' £4 'hassle-free' flowers with 'charming' year-round feature
News.content:
Home Bargains' £4 'hassle-free' flowers with 'charming' year-round feature

The discount sotre's 'flowers' add a 'delightful pop of colour' to gardens

View 3 Images These 'flowers' add 'a touch of beauty' to gardens all year round (Image: Peter Dazeley/Getty Images)

Home Bargains shoppers can add a "hassle-free" way of brightening up their flowerbeds, pathways, or patios this spring with new 'flowers' that add more than just a "delightful pop of colour". These solar-powered 'flowers' bring a year-round "glow" to outdoor spaces.

‌



The Firefly Stake Lights cost £3.99 each and come in three styles. Shoppers can choose a vibrant yellow sunflower or one of two types of delicate roses with pink or red petals, which look realistic when 'planted' in gardens.

‌



Describing the floral lights online, a Home Bargains spokesperson said: "Add a touch of beauty to your garden with the Firefly Solar Stake Lights. These charming floral lights automatically turn on at dusk, casting a warm LED white glow that enhances your outdoor space.

‌



"Easy to install, they come with a rechargeable battery for eco-friendly, hassle-free use. Perfect for flowerbeds, pathways, or patios, these lights bring a delightful pop of colour and light to your garden every evening. Enjoy the warmth and elegance of these stunning solar stake lights year-round!"

The solar lights are available to order online, but shoppers won't be able to choose which kind of 'flower' they get, they'll receive one of the three options at random. To make sure their garden vision is exactly as planned, shoppers should look for these items in stores.

View 3 Images Each flower style is sold separately for £3.99 (Image: Home Bargains)

‌



Home Bargains has close to 600 stores across the UK, including England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. People can find their nearest by using the online postcode tool here.

There are similar styles of lights available elsewhere, such as Amazon's FORUP Solar Garden Stake Lights, which look like realistic sunflowers and stretch up to 40cm tall. The two-pack of 'flowers' is available to order online for £23.52.

Shoppers could also opt for something else, like the Set of Two Solar Hyacinth Stake Lights from The Range. This £14.99 set comes in clusters of pink and purple flowers that "glow for up to six hours on a full charge".

‌



View 3 Images The discount store has updated its outdoor lighting range (Image: Getty)

In other Home Bargains news, shoppers are not only thinking about enhancing their outdoor spaces but also their interiors. People have been impressed by some of the furniture and decor the discount store has brought out in recent weeks.

For example, a £10 'timeless' looking lamp on offer at Home Bargains could easily be mistaken for one worth almost £40 at B&Q. The Home Collections Ceramic Esme Lamp has a quite similar look to the Capan Crackled Round Natural Distressed-effect Table Lamp from B&Q, which is worth £38.

Article continues below

And, another interior find included a 'chic' £30 rug with a 'luxury' and 'modern' aesthetic that was said to be 'perfect for any room' - and looked very much like a more expensive item on sale at M&S. The £30 rug could be easily mistaken for Marks and Spencer's Anders Rug from Asiatic, which costs £109.

News.title: 'Make laundry day easier' with Dunelm's 'sturdy' and 'practical' £8 solution
News.content:
'Make laundry day easier' with Dunelm's 'sturdy' and 'practical' £8 solution

The homeware retailer says the new £8 product is 'perfect for busy households'

View 3 Images The laundry basket is designed to save space in bathrooms and kitchens (Image: Dunelm)

Dunelm says shoppers can 'make laundry day easier' and keep their home organised with a new £8 item. The high street retailer is stocking a 'practical' laundry basket with two distinctive features.

‌



Unlike most baskets, Dunelm's new 'sturdy' solution attaches to the wall in your bathroom. It also 'folds flat when not in use' to save space. According to the retailer, the design makes the basket 'perfect for busy households'. The store is selling the Collapsible Wall Large Laundry Basket for £8 on its website.

‌



Shoppers interested in the basket can buy it for click-and-collect. The basket measures H 34cm x W 25cm x D 48cm, and the design is white with grey accents.

‌



Showcasing the new laundry basket to shoppers, Dunelm wrote: "Make laundry day easier and your home more organised with the Collapsible Wall Large Laundry Basket. Perfect for busy households, this clever design folds flat when not in use, saving valuable space in your utility room or bathroom.

"Featuring a built-in hanging hole, it’s simple to store, just hang it up when you’re done. Lightweight yet sturdy, this basket is ideal for everyday laundry tasks and offers a practical solution for smaller spaces without compromising on capacity."

View 3 Images Dunelm's Collapsible Wall Large Laundry Basket (Image: Dunelm)

‌



The product is new on Dunelm's website, and there are no reviews at the time of writing. However, shoppers who want to read reviews before making a purchase have plenty of other options from the retailer.

For £14, Dunelm offers the Global Slim Laundry Hamper. The description says: "Make the most of your bathroom space with this slim laundry hamper, designed to fit neatly into smaller areas.

"The patterned finish brings a modern touch, while the foldable design makes it easy to store away when not in use. Lightweight yet practical, it’s perfect for keeping laundry organised without taking up too much room. A hinged lid helps keep everything out of sight for a tidy look. Ideal for everyday use in compact bathrooms."

‌



View 3 Images Dunelm says the product is 'perfect for busy households' (Image: Dunelm)

A happy shopper said: "Is slim so fitted into a narrow space, tall so can accommodate a lot of washing. Sturdy build and the lid stops dust getting in smells getting out." But someone else claimed: "Great size for what I needed and colour but poorly designed. Had to get some wood to support bottom and side as collapsed when it is empty."

For £35, there's also the Modern Luxe Ribbed Velvet Slimline Laundry Basket. Dunelm says: "Bring effortless style to your laundry storage with this Modern Luxe Ribbed Velvet Laundry Basket. With a slimline shape and a lid to keep things tidy, it is great for compact bathrooms and won't overwhelm your floor space.

Article continues below

"The ribbed velvet finish gives a luxurious feel and it would be the perfect addition to a modern or boho interior design style." A reviewer agreed: "Beautiful fabric. Nice and narrow to fit into small spaces. Perfect."

News.title: King Charles poses for photo with Canadian PM Mark Carney and people spot awkward detail
News.content:
King Charles poses for photo with Canadian PM Mark Carney and people spot awkward detail

Everyone's talking about a picture of King Charles and the Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney at Buckingham Palace – and it's probably not for the reason you're expecting

View 2 Images King Charles III greets Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney during an audience at Buckingham Palace in London on March 16 (Image: POOL/AFP via Getty Images)

King Charles hosted Mark Carney at Buckingham Palace on the Canadian Prime Minister's 61st birthday – but that's not the detail that caught everyone's attention.

‌



The King met with the world leader on March 16 – around nine months after delivering the Speech from the Throne to Canada. In the address, he promoted "unity, stability and continuity" between the two nations and beyond.

‌



So it's no surprise the pair seemed at ease with one another as they shook hands, chatted comfortably and posed for photographs in the 1844 Room.

‌



However, it's not just the friendly body language that has sparked discussion. When the Royal Family's official X page posted about the meeting, people were quick to spot an amusing detail.

Content cannot be displayed without consent

Amusingly, an ornament in the background made it look like an exotic bird was perched on Mark Carney's blazer.

‌



One X user admitted: "I genuinely thought Mark Carney had a parrot on his shoulder." Another giggled: "Haha! That's an interesting illusion."

A third wrote: "That is hilarious." And a fourth added: "I hadn't noticed it before – but now that you mention it, I can't unsee it."

It's not the only time the parrot has photobombed King Charles. It also ruffled feathers when His Majesty shook hands with Ukraine's President Volodymyr Zelensky during a private audience at Buckingham Palace in London on March 17, 2026.

‌



View 2 Images The exotic bird has stolen the show on multiple occasions (Image: POOL/AFP via Getty Images)

Even though the ornamental parrot is a hot topic of conversation, the Canadian PM's trip to the UK seems to be a very productive one.

As well as attending an engagement with the King, Carney discussed the conflict in Ukraine with Sir Keir Starmer at No10 on Monday.

‌



A government spokesperson said: "The leaders began by discussing the strength of the UK-Canada relationship, noting that ties between the two countries are closer than ever, including deep cooperation on trade and defence.

"They agreed that there is significant scope to build further on this partnership, delivering for people both in the UK and in Canada.

"They discussed the situation in the Middle East and agreed on the importance of restoring freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz, underlining the need for stability and the protection of international shipping routes.

Article continues below

"The leaders also underscored their commitment to Ukraine and reiterated the need for all partners to maintain pressure on Russia and its war chest.They looked forward to speaking again soon."

News.title: Club Chemistry staff member diagnosed with meningitis as Kent outbreak spreads
News.content:
Club Chemistry staff member diagnosed with meningitis as Kent outbreak spreads

Louise Jones-Roberts, the owner of Club Chemistry, said she believed there could have been more than one case that could be connected to people meeting at the venue

View 3 Images University of Kent students have been queuing for antibiotics (Image: PA)

A staff member at Club Chemistry has been confirmed to have meningitis as the Kent outbreak continues to spread.

‌



The owner of the nightclub said a staff member contracted the illness but is doing well. Some 2,000 people would have visited the venue over the three dates that have been identified by health officials as when people are believed to have been infected.

‌



Louise Jones-Roberts, the owner of Club Chemistry, said she believed there could be more than one case that was connected to mingling at the club and she was told she had "been told they started showing symptoms on March 10".

‌



View 3 Images The club is believed to be the site where people became infected with meningitis (Image: Getty Images)

British mum in coma with meningitis in Tenerife after sudden headache at airport READ MORE :

She added nearly all staff members have been traced and told to get antibiotics, reports LBC. She continued: "I just really hope people know the symptoms and no more lives are lost."

The club has since closed and will remain so until further notice. Health officials have said Meningitis B has been identified as the strain behind the recent cases.

‌



Health officials have said 13 cases have been reported since Friday. The UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA) said fast-acting infection Meningitis B is the specific strain in some of the cases that have been monitored.

Emergency measures are now being take to prevent the epidemic before students disperse across the UK over the Easter break. Experts said the outbreak has spread from Canterbury with cases being reported in the nearby towns of Ashford and Whitstable.

MenB is the most common type of meningitis infection that affects protective membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord in the UK. The UK has rolled out vaccination against MenB for babies and young children since 2015.

‌



View 3 Images Health officials are concerned about how many people have been affected by the outbreak (Image: Tony Kershaw / SWNS)

Health chiefs have since said the meningitis crisis is a once in a generation outbreak event. Students have rushed to get antibiotics to students who were at risk of falling ill.

The NHS had initially managed the cases as a major regional incident but they have now increased that overlay to having a national-level oversight as well. The UK Health Security Agency is now mobilising to quickly treat any students with antibiotics before they fall ill after two students realised too late and died.

Article continues below

UKHSA CEO Susan Hopkins said: “This looks like a super spreader event with ongoing spread within the halls of residents in the universities. There will have been some parties particularly around this so there will have been lots of social mixing.

“I can’t yet say where the initial infection came from, how it’s got into this cohort, and why it’s created such an explosive amount of infections. I can say that, in my 35 years working in medicine, in healthcare and hospitals, this is the most cases I’ve seen in a single weekend with this type of infection."

News.content: "The situation is frightening…," says Ali. "The skies of your country are controlled by enemy forces. But at the same time there is always a hope in people's hearts. But hoping simply that for one moment, something might happen that ends the current Iranian regime, and that the people will be able to create change."

News.title: Morocco awarded Africa Cup of Nations title after Senegal win overturned
News.content:
Morocco have been declared the winners of the 2025 Africa Cup of Nations after the Confederation of African Football (Caf) overturned the result of the final following Senegal's controversial walk-off.

Senegal beat Morocco 1-0 in the final on 18 January in a match that was overshadowed when the Senegalese players refused to play after the hosts were awarded a stoppage-time penalty with the match goalless.

Following a delay of about 17 minutes, the players did eventually return and Brahim Diaz's penalty was saved before Senegal's Pape Gueye scored an extra-time winner.

However, that result has now been overturned after a decision by an appeal board at African football's governing body.

A statement from Caf said Senegal had been "declared to have forfeited the final match" with the "result of the match being recorded as 3-0 in favour" of Morocco.

The Senegalese Football Federation said it would appeal against Caf's decision at the Court of Arbitration for Sport, calling it "an unfair, unprecedented and unacceptable decision which brings discredit to African football".

The decision follows an appeal by the Moroccan FA (FRMF) which Caf said was "declared admissible in form" and was upheld.

All of these are experiments in which, historically, the UK played a leading role. But now, British scientists could be largely locked out of them in the future, if the STFC does not pay its way.
